title: Share of land defined as natural habitat
subtitle: Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.
caption:
Share of land defined as natural habitat
Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.

The line chart tracks the percentage of total land area classified as natural habitat from 1780 to 2010, covering the period before and after the industrial era. The vertical axis measures the share of land from 0% to 80%, while the horizontal axis spans over two centuries. Four distinct lines represent the World average alongside Germany, France, and the United Kingdom. The global trend line begins at approximately 75% in 1780 and follows a consistent downward trajectory, reaching roughly 50% by 2010. In contrast, the European nations display a U-shaped pattern. The United Kingdom starts with the highest share among the three countries at roughly 64% in 1780, then declines steadily to a minimum of roughly 29% around 1940, before rising to approximately 48% by 2010. Germany and France begin lower, near 46% and 49% respectively, and both dip to lows between 40% and 45% in the early 20th century. From the mid-20th century onward, both Germany and France show a steep increase, with Germany reaching roughly 66% and France reaching roughly 63% by 2010. The data shows that while the global share of natural habitat has decreased over time, these specific European countries have seen a reversal of that trend in recent decades, with Germany and France returning to levels higher than their 1780 starting points. The divergence between the continuous global decline and the recent recovery in these national figures represents the most visible structural feature of the dataset.
